planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in blocks (minimum 4 rows x 10 feet) for adequate wind pollination; Golden Bantam was introduced in 1902 by W. Atlee Burpee and was the first commercially successful yellow sweet corn in America — before its introduction, virtually all sweet corn was white, and Golden Bantam's popularity transformed consumer preference toward yellow corn that persists to this day.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks (not single rows) to ensure adequate cross-pollination by wind; plant in succession every 2–3 weeks for continuous harvest. Golden Bantam was introduced in 1902 by W. Atlee Burpee's seed company and was arguably the most historically significant sweet corn introduction of the 20th century — before 1902, virtually all sweet corn grown in America was white, and Golden Bantam's immediate popularity as the first widely available yellow sweet corn variety permanently shifted American consumer preference toward yellow corn. The short (5–6 foot) plants produce 7–8 inch ears with 8 rows of golden-yellow kernels; the flavor is distinctively old-fashioned sweet corn flavor — less sugary than modern hybrid types but with a rich, genuine corn taste many find more satisfying and more compatible with cooking (when cooked, the sugars of old SU types convert to starch more completely, giving a proper corn texture). Harvest at milk stage (18–21 days after silk), when kernels exude milky fluid when pressed; use immediately as sugars convert rapidly.

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks after last frost; Country Gentleman is an 1890 white sweet corn heirloom with the distinctive 'shoepeg' kernel arrangement — narrow, crowded, irregular-rowed white kernels packed so densely they have no defined row pattern — producing exceptionally sweet, tender, richly flavored corn considered by many heirloom growers to be the finest-flavored of all old sweet corn varieties.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks of at least 4 rows for wind pollination; isolate from other corn by 250–400 feet or by planting to mature 14 days apart to prevent cross-pollination. Country Gentleman sweet corn dates to 1890 and is a distinctive old SU (standard sugary) type characterized by its 'shoepeg' kernel arrangement — the narrow, elongated, irregular-spaced white kernels are packed so closely they fill the space between rows and lack the defined row pattern of normal corn, a characteristic shared with Stowell's Evergreen and a handful of other white heirloom corns. This unique kernel arrangement has been prized by cooks for generations because the irregular corn can be packed more densely into cans and jars; Country Gentleman shoepeg corn canned at home was a Southern kitchen staple for much of the 20th century. The flavor is rich, complex, and exceptionally sweet for an old-type corn. The variety is a late-season type (88–95 days) suited to long-season gardens; the tall plants (6–8 feet) are vigorous and productive.

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks after last frost; Silver Queen was introduced in 1955 and rapidly became the most popular sweet corn in the Eastern United States, prized for its large, tender, exceptionally sweet white kernels — it dominated the Eastern US sweet corn market for decades and remains the benchmark white sweet corn against which all others are measured.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks after last frost; Silver Queen is a standard SU type that requires warm soil (65°F+) for good germination and will not tolerate cold planting conditions that sugary enhanced (SE) and supersweet (SH2) types also struggle with. Silver Queen sweet corn was introduced in 1955 and is arguably the most important and influential white sweet corn variety in American history, dominating Eastern US markets and home gardens from the 1960s through the 1980s and remaining widely grown today; the large (8–9 inch), 14–16 row ears produce exceptionally tender, plump, snow-white kernels with a creamy sweetness and satisfying corn flavor that became the Eastern standard for excellence. Silver Queen is an SU (standard sugary) type that holds in the field only 1–2 days after reaching peak milk stage — its brief harvest window is part of its legendary status, as freshly-picked Silver Queen at peak ripeness justifies all the hype while overripe or old Silver Queen is disappointing. Longer maturity (90–95 days) means it needs a long season; excellent in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens when planted in early spring.
sources: Virginia Cooperative Extension Vegetable Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks after last frost; Jubilee is a midseason yellow sweet corn with excellent flavor, good disease resistance, and adaptability to both Northern and Southern climates — a reliable, high-quality variety that performs well throughout zones 4–11 and is particularly well-suited to Gulf Coast gardens where it can be planted for both spring and fall corn seasons.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks; Jubilee is an SU type that benefits from block planting for good pollination and is best planted at least 400 feet from supersweet (SH2) types to prevent cross-pollination that reduces sweetness. Jubilee is a midseason (82–87 days) yellow sweet corn that has been widely grown in American gardens and markets since the 1950s and maintains a reputation for reliable production, excellent flavor, and adaptability to a wide range of climates from the Upper Midwest to the Gulf Coast; the large ears (8–9 inches) carry 14–16 rows of bright golden-yellow kernels with classic, rich sweet corn flavor — the balance of sweetness and traditional corn taste that makes fresh summer sweet corn a culinary experience distinct from the excessive sweetness of modern hybrid types. Jubilee has better disease resistance than many older varieties (including some tolerance to Stewart's wilt and common rust) and holds slightly longer in the field than older SU types. Outstanding in Gulf Coast gardens for both the spring crop (April–May planting) and fall crop (late July–August planting).

common_name: Peaches and Cream Sweet Corn (Bicolor Sweet Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks after last frost; Peaches and Cream is the classic American bicolor sweet corn with a mixture of white and yellow kernels on each ear — a SE (augmented sugary) type with better sweetness retention than standard SU varieties and one of the most widely sold sweet corn varieties in American garden catalogs for the past 40 years.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks of at least 4 rows in warm soil; Peaches and Cream is an SE type that holds sweetness in the field 2–3 days longer than standard SU varieties and in the refrigerator up to 5 days, giving more flexibility than older corn types. Peaches and Cream is an SE (sugary enhanced) type bicolor sweet corn that became one of the most popular garden corn varieties in America from the 1980s onward, combining the classic appeal of mixed yellow-and-white kernels on each ear with the improved sweetness retention of the SE gene; the creamy yellow and white kernel mix in each ear produces both the pure sweetness of white corn and the fuller flavor of yellow corn simultaneously. The SE gene gives kernels a longer window of peak sweetness than standard SU types — important for home gardeners who cannot harvest at the exact moment of peak ripeness. The ears are 7–8 inches with 16–18 rows; plants are medium-height (5–6.5 feet) and compact. Excellent for home gardens throughout the country; available in many open-pollinated and hybrid forms (most sold commercially are hybrids).
sources: University of Illinois Extension Vegetable Guide
